text.Rotherham: And her spirit returned, and she rose up instantly, and he ordered that something should be given her to eat.

text.NRSV: Her parents were astounded; but he ordered them to tell no one what had happened.
text.Rotherham: And her parents were beside themselves. But, he, charged them to tell, no one, what had happened.

text.NRSV: Then Jesus called the twelve together and gave them power and authority over all demons and to cure diseases,
text.Rotherham: And, calling together the twelve, he gave them power and authority over all the demons, and to be curing, diseases;

text.NRSV: and he sent them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal.
text.Rotherham: and sent them forth, to be proclaiming the kingdom of God, and to be healing;

text.NRSV: He said to them, “Take nothing for your journey, no staff, nor bag, nor bread, nor money — not even an extra tunic.
text.Rotherham: and said unto them—Nothing, take ye for the journey,—neither staff, nor satchel, nor bread, nor silver, nor to have, two tunics.

text.NRSV: Whatever house you enter, stay there, and leave from there.
text.Rotherham: And, into whatsoever house ye enter, there, abide, and, thence, be going forth.

text.NRSV: Wherever they do not welcome you, as you are leaving that town shake the dust off your feet as a testimony against them.”
text.Rotherham: And, as many soever as shall not welcome you, in going forth from that city, the dust of your feet, shake ye off, for a witness against them.

text.NRSV: They departed and went through the villages, bringing the good news and curing diseases everywhere.
text.Rotherham: And they went forth, and were passing through, along the villages,—delivering the glad-message, and effecting cures in every direction.

text.NRSV: Now Herod the ruler heard about all that had taken place, and he was perplexed, because it was said by some that John had been raised from the dead,
text.Rotherham: Now Herod the tetrarch heard of all the things which were coming to pass, and was utterly at a loss, because of its being said, by some, that, John, had been raised from the dead;

text.NRSV: by some that Elijah had appeared, and by others that one of the ancient prophets had arisen.
text.Rotherham: and, by some, that, Elijah, had appeared; and, by others, that, some prophet of the ancients, had arisen.

text.NRSV: Herod said, “John I beheaded; but who is this about whom I hear such things?” And he tried to see him.
text.Rotherham: But Herod said—John, I myself, beheaded; but, who is this, concerning whom I am hearing such things as these? And he was seeking to see him.

text.NRSV: On their return the apostles told Jesus all they had done. He took them with him and withdrew privately to a city called Bethsaida.
text.Rotherham: And the Apostles, returning, related to him what great things they had done. And, taking them aside, he retired privately, into a city called Bethsaida.

text.NRSV: When the crowds found out about it, they followed him; and he welcomed them, and spoke to them about the kingdom of God, and healed those who needed to be cured.
text.Rotherham: But, the multitudes, getting to know, followed him; and, giving them welcome, he began speaking unto them concerning the kingdom of God,—and, them that had need of cure, he healed,

text.NRSV: The day was drawing to a close, and the twelve came to him and said, “Send the crowd away, so that they may go into the surrounding villages and countryside, to lodge and get provisions; for we are here in a deserted place.”
text.Rotherham: And, the day, began to decline; and the twelve, coming near, said to him—Dismiss the multitude, that, going into the surrounding villages and hamlets, they may lodge, and find provisions; because, here, in a desert place, are we.

text.NRSV: But he said to them, “You give them something to eat.” They said, “We have no more than five loaves and two fish — unless we are to go and buy food for all these people.”
text.Rotherham: But he said unto them—Give, ye, them to eat. They, however, said—We have not more than five loaves, and two fishes,—unless perhaps, we, should go, and buy food, for all this people.

text.NRSV: For there were about five thousand men. And he said to his disciples, “Make them sit down in groups of about fifty each.”
text.Rotherham: For there were about five thousand men. And he said unto his disciples—Make them recline, in companies of about fifty each.

text.NRSV: They did so and made them all sit down.
text.Rotherham: And they did so,—and made, one and all, recline.

text.NRSV: And taking the five loaves and the two fish, he looked up to heaven, and blessed and broke them, and gave them to the disciples to set before the crowd.
text.Rotherham: And, taking the five loaves, and the two fishes, looking up into heaven, he blessed them, and brake [them] up, and went on giving to the disciples, to set before the multitude;

text.NRSV: And all ate and were filled. What was left over was gathered up, twelve baskets of broken pieces.
text.Rotherham: and they did all eat and were filled. And that which remained over to them, was taken up,—of broken pieces, twelve baskets.

text.NRSV: Once when Jesus was praying alone, with only the disciples near him, he asked them, “Who do the crowds say that I am?”
text.Rotherham: And, it came to pass, when he was praying in solitude, the disciples, were with him. And he questioned them, saying—Who do the multitudes say that I am?

text.NRSV: They answered, “John the Baptist; but others, Elijah; and still others, that one of the ancient prophets has arisen.”
text.Rotherham: And they, answering, said John the Immerser; But some, Elijah, and others, that, some prophet of the ancients, hath arisen.

text.NRSV: He said to them, “But who do you say that I am?” Peter answered, “The Messiah of God.”
text.Rotherham: And he said to them—But who say, ye, that I am? And, Peter, answering, said—The Christ of God!

text.NRSV: He sternly ordered and commanded them not to tell anyone,
text.Rotherham: He, however, sternly admonishing them, gave charge that, unto no one, should they be telling this;

text.NRSV: saying, “The Son of Man must undergo great suffering, and be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and scribes, and be killed, and on the third day be raised.”
text.Rotherham: saying—The Son of Man must needs suffer, many things, and be rejected by the Elders and High priests and Scribes, and be slain,—and, on the third day, arise.

text.NRSV: Then he said to them all, “If any want to become my followers, let them deny themselves and take up their cross daily and follow me.
text.Rotherham: And he was saying, unto all—If any one intendeth after me to come, Let him deny himself, and take up his cross daily,—and be following me.

text.NRSV: For those who want to save their life will lose it, and those who lose their life for my sake will save it.
text.Rotherham: For, whosoever intendeth, his life, to save, shall lose it; but, whosoever shall lose his life for my sake, the same, shall save it?

text.NRSV: What does it profit them if they gain the whole world, but lose or forfeit themselves?
text.Rotherham: For what doth a man profit, who hath gained the whole world, but hath lost, or been made to forfeit, himself?

text.NRSV: Those who are ashamed of me and of my words, of them the Son of Man will be ashamed when he comes in his glory and the glory of the Father and of the holy angels.
text.Rotherham: For, whosoever shall be ashamed of me, and of my words, of him, the Son of Man, will be ashamed, whensoever he shall come in his glory, and that of the Father, and of the holy messengers.

text.NRSV: But truly I tell you, there are some standing here who will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God.”
text.Rotherham: But I tell you of a truth—There, are, some of those, here, standing, who shall in nowise taste of death, until they see the kingdom of God.

text.NRSV: Now about eight days after these sayings Jesus took with him Peter and John and James, and went up on the mountain to pray.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, after these words, about eight days, taking with him Peter and John and James, he went up into the mountain to pray.

text.NRSV: And while he was praying, the appearance of his face changed, and his clothes became dazzling white.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, while he was praying, that, the appearance of his face, was changed, and, his clothing, became white, effulgent.

text.NRSV: Suddenly they saw two men, Moses and Elijah, talking to him.
text.Rotherham: And lo! two men, were conversing with him, who, indeed, were Moses and Elijah,—

text.NRSV: They appeared in glory and were speaking of his departure, which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: who, appearing in glory, were speaking as to his departure, which he was about to fulfil in Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Now Peter and his companions were weighed down with sleep; but since they had stayed awake, they saw his glory and the two men who stood with him.
text.Rotherham: Now, Peter, and they who were with him, had become heavy with sleep; but, waking up, they saw his glory, and the two men who were standing with him.

text.NRSV: Just as they were leaving him, Peter said to Jesus, “Master, it is good for us to be here; let us make three dwellings, one for you, one for Moses, and one for Elijah” — not knowing what he said.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when they were being parted from him, Peter said unto Jesus—Master! it is, delightful, for us to be, here: Let us, therefore, make three tents, one for thee, and one for Moses, and one for Elijah:—not knowing what he said.

text.NRSV: While he was saying this, a cloud came and overshadowed them; and they were terrified as they entered the cloud.
text.Rotherham: Now, while he was saying these things, there came a cloud, and it began to overshadow them,—and they were overcome with fear as they entered into the cloud.

text.NRSV: Then from the cloud came a voice that said, “This is my Son, my Chosen; listen to him!”
text.Rotherham: And, a voice, came out of the cloud, saying—This, is my Son, the Chosen One: Unto him, be hearkening.

text.NRSV: When the voice had spoken, Jesus was found alone. And they kept silent and in those days told no one any of the things they had seen.
text.Rotherham: And, when the voice came, there was found, Jesus alone. And they held their peace, and, unto no one, reported they, in those days, any of the things which they had seen.

text.NRSV: On the next day, when they had come down from the mountain, a great crowd met him.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, on the next day, when they came down from the mountain, that there met him a great multitude.

text.NRSV: Just then a man from the crowd shouted, “Teacher, I beg you to look at my son; he is my only child.
text.Rotherham: And lo! a man from the multitude, uttered a cry, saying—Teacher! I beg of thee, to look upon my son, because, my only begotten, he is.

text.NRSV: Suddenly a spirit seizes him, and all at once he shrieks; it convulses him until he foams at the mouth; it mauls him and will scarcely leave him.
text.Rotherham: And lo! a spirit, taketh him, and, suddenly, he crieth out,—and it convulseth him—with foaming, and with difficulty departeth from him, sorely bruising him.

text.NRSV: I begged your disciples to cast it out, but they could not.”
text.Rotherham: And I begged of thy disciples, that they would cast it out, and they could not.

text.NRSV: Jesus answered, “You faithless and perverse generation, how much longer must I be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here.”
text.Rotherham: And, making answer, Jesus said—O faithless and perverted generation! how long shall I be with you, and bear with you? Bring, hither, thy son.

text.NRSV: While he was coming, the demon dashed him to the ground in convulsions. But Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, healed the boy, and gave him back to his father.
text.Rotherham: And, while yet he was coming, the demon tare him, and mangled him. But Jesus rebuked the impure spirit, and healed the boy, and gave him back to his father.

text.NRSV: And all were astounded at the greatness of God. While everyone was amazed at all that he was doing, he said to his disciples,
text.Rotherham: And they were all being struck with astonishment, at the majesty of God. And, while all were marvelling at all things which he was doing, he said unto his disciples:

text.NRSV: “Let these words sink into your ears: The Son of Man is going to be betrayed into human hands.”
text.Rotherham: Lay, ye, up in your ears, these words; for, the Son of Man, is about to be delivered up, into the hands of men.

text.NRSV: But they did not understand this saying; its meaning was concealed from them, so that they could not perceive it. And they were afraid to ask him about this saying.
text.Rotherham: But, they, understood not this saying, and it had become veiled from them, that they might not grasp it,—and they were afraid to question him, concerning this saying.

text.NRSV: An argument arose among them as to which one of them was the greatest.
text.Rotherham: But there entered a reasoning among them, as to who of them should be greatest.

text.NRSV: But Jesus, aware of their inner thoughts, took a little child and put it by his side,
text.Rotherham: And, Jesus, perceiving the reasoning of their heart, taking a child, placed it near himself,—

text.NRSV: and said to them, “Whoever welcomes this child in my name welcomes me, and whoever welcomes me welcomes the one who sent me; for the least among all of you is the greatest.”
text.Rotherham: and said unto them—Whosoever shall give welcome unto this child, on my name, unto me, giveth welcome; and, whosoever, unto me, giveth welcome, giveth welcome unto him that sent me forth. For, he who is least among you all, the same, is, great.

text.NRSV: John answered, “Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name, and we tried to stop him, because he does not follow with us.”
text.Rotherham: But John, answering, said—Master! we saw some one, in thy name, casting out demons,—and we forbade him, because he followeth not with us.

text.NRSV: But Jesus said to him, “Do not stop him; for whoever is not against you is for you.”
text.Rotherham: And Jesus said unto him—Do not forbid; for, whosoever is not against you, is, for you.

text.NRSV: When the days drew near for him to be taken up, he set his face to go to Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when the days for taking him up were on the point of being fulfilled, even he himself, set, his face, to be journeying unto Jerusalem;

text.NRSV: And he sent messengers ahead of him. On their way they entered a village of the Samaritans to make ready for him;
text.Rotherham: and he sent messengers before his face,—and, taking their journey, they entered into a village of Samaritans, so as to prepare for him.

text.NRSV: but they did not receive him, because his face was set toward Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: And they welcomed him not, because, his face, was for journeying unto Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: When his disciples James and John saw it, they said, “Lord, do you want us to command fire to come down from heaven and consume them?”
text.Rotherham: And the disciples James and John, seeing it, said—Lord! wilt thou, that we bid fire come down from heaven, and destroy them?

text.NRSV: But he turned and rebuked them.
text.Rotherham: But, turning, he rebuked them.

text.NRSV: Then they went on to another village.
text.Rotherham: And they journeyed into a different village.

text.NRSV: As they were going along the road, someone said to him, “I will follow you wherever you go.”
text.Rotherham: And, as they were journeying on the road, one said unto him—I will follow thee, whithersoever thou shalt depart.

text.NRSV: And Jesus said to him, “Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests; but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.”
text.Rotherham: And Jesus said unto him—the foxes, have, dens, and, the birds of heaven, nests; but, the Son of Man, hath not where, his head, he may recline.

text.NRSV: To another he said, “Follow me.” But he said, “Lord, first let me go and bury my father.”
text.Rotherham: And he said unto another—Be following me! But, he, said—Suffer me, first, to depart, and bury my father.

text.NRSV: But Jesus said to him, “Let the dead bury their own dead; but as for you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God.”
text.Rotherham: And he said unto him—Leave, the dead, to bury their own dead; but, thou, departing, be declaring the kingdom of God.

text.NRSV: Another said, “I will follow you, Lord; but let me first say farewell to those at my home.”
text.Rotherham: And, yet another, said—I will follow thee, Lord; But, first, suffer me to bid adieu to them that are in my house.

text.NRSV: Jesus said to him, “No one who puts a hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God.”
text.Rotherham: But Jesus said [unto him] —No one, laying the hand on a plough and looking unto the things behind, is, fit, for the kingdom of God.

text.NRSV: After this the Lord appointed seventy others and sent them on ahead of him in pairs to every town and place where he himself intended to go.
text.Rotherham: And, after these things, the Lord appointed seventy two others, and sent them forth, two and two before his face, into every city and place whither, he himself, was about to come.

text.NRSV: He said to them, “The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few; therefore ask the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers into his harvest.
text.Rotherham: And he was saying unto them—The harvest, indeed, is, great, but, the labourers, few; beg ye, therefore, of the Lord of the harvest, that he would thrust forth, labourers, into his harvest.

text.NRSV: Go on your way. See, I am sending you out like lambs into the midst of wolves.
text.Rotherham: Withdraw! Lo! I send you forth as lambs amid wolves.

text.NRSV: Carry no purse, no bag, no sandals; and greet no one on the road.
text.Rotherham: Be not carrying purse, or satchel, or sandals, and, no one along the road, salute ye;

text.NRSV: Whatever house you enter, first say, ‘Peace to this house!’
text.Rotherham: And, into whatsoever house ye enter, First, say, Peace to this house!

text.NRSV: And if anyone is there who shares in peace, your peace will rest on that person; but if not, it will return to you.
text.Rotherham: And, if the son of peace be, there, your peace, shall rest upon it; but, otherwise, at least, unto you, shall it return.

text.NRSV: Stay in the same house, eating and drinking whatever they provide, for the laborer deserves to be paid. Do not move about from house to house.
text.Rotherham: And, in the self-same house, abide ye, eating and drinking such things as they have; for, worthy, is the labourer, of his hire: be not removing from house to house.

text.NRSV: Whenever you enter a town and its people welcome you, eat what is set before you;
text.Rotherham: And, into whatsoever city ye shall enter, and they bid you welcome, be eating such things as are set before you;

text.NRSV: cure the sick who are there, and say to them, ‘The kingdom of God has come near to you.’
text.Rotherham: And be curing the sick that are, therein, and be saying unto them—The kingdom of God hath drawn nigh upon you.

text.NRSV: But whenever you enter a town and they do not welcome you, go out into its streets and say,
text.Rotherham: But, into whatsoever city ye shall enter, and they do not welcome you, going forth into the broadways thereof, say ye:

text.NRSV: ‘Even the dust of your town that clings to our feet, we wipe off in protest against you. Yet know this: the kingdom of God has come near.’
text.Rotherham: Even the dust that cleaveth unto us, out of your city, unto our feet, do we wipe off against you; nevertheless, of this, be taking notice—The kingdom of God hath drawn near.

text.NRSV: I tell you, on that day it will be more tolerable for Sodom than for that town.
text.Rotherham: I tell you, for them of Sodom, in that day, more tolerable, will it be, than for that city.

text.NRSV: “Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the deeds of power done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.
text.Rotherham: Alas for thee, Chorazin! Alas for thee, Bethsaida! Because, if, in Tyre and Zidon, had been done the works of power which have been down in you, of old, in sackcloth and ashes sitting, they would have repented.

text.NRSV: But at the judgment it will be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon than for you.
text.Rotherham: Moreover, for Tyre and Zidon, more tolerable, will it be, in the judgment, than, for you.

text.NRSV: And you, Capernaum, will you be exalted to heaven? No, you will be brought down to Hades.
text.Rotherham: And, thou, Capernaum,—Unto heaven, shalt thou be uplifted? …Unto hades, thou shalt be brought down!

text.NRSV: “Whoever listens to you listens to me, and whoever rejects you rejects me, and whoever rejects me rejects the one who sent me.”
text.Rotherham: He that hearkeneth unto you, unto me, doth hearken, and, he that setteth you aside, doth set, me, aside; and, he that setteth, me, aside, doth set aside, him that sent me.

text.NRSV: The seventy returned with joy, saying, “Lord, in your name even the demons submit to us!”
text.Rotherham: And the seventy [-two] returned, with joy, saying—Lord! even the demons, submit themselves unto us, in thy name!

text.NRSV: He said to them, “I watched Satan fall from heaven like a flash of lightning.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them—I was beholding Satan, when, like lightning, out of heaven, he fell!

text.NRSV: See, I have given you authority to tread on snakes and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy; and nothing will hurt you.
text.Rotherham: Lo! I have given you the authority—to be treading upon serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy, and, nothing, unto you, shall in anywise do harm;

text.NRSV: Nevertheless, do not rejoice at this, that the spirits submit to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.”
text.Rotherham: Notwithstanding, in this, be not rejoicing—that, the spirits, unto you submit themselves; but be rejoicing—that, your names, are inscribed in the heavens!

text.NRSV: At that same hour Jesus rejoiced in the Holy Spirit and said, “I thank you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you have hidden these things from the wise and the intelligent and have revealed them to infants; yes, Father, for such was your gracious will.
text.Rotherham: In the self-same hour, exulted he in the Holy Spirit, and said—I openly give praise unto thee, Father! Lord of heaven and earth! in that thou hast hid these things from the wise and discerning, and hast revealed them unto babes,—Yea, O Father! that, so, hath it become, a delight, before thee.

text.NRSV: All things have been handed over to me by my Father; and no one knows who the Son is except the Father, or who the Father is except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.”
text.Rotherham: All things, unto me, have been delivered up by my Father; and, no one, knoweth, who the Son is, save the Father,—and who the Father is, save the Son, and he to whomsoever the Son may be minded to reveal him.

text.NRSV: Then turning to the disciples, Jesus said to them privately, “Blessed are the eyes that see what you see!
text.Rotherham: And, turning unto his disciples, privately, he said—Happy, the eyes, that see what ye see!

text.NRSV: For I tell you that many prophets and kings desired to see what you see, but did not see it, and to hear what you hear, but did not hear it.”
text.Rotherham: For I tell you, Many prophets and kings, have desired to see what, ye, see, and they saw not, and to hear what ye hear, and they heard not.

text.NRSV: Just then a lawyer stood up to test Jesus. “Teacher,” he said, “what must I do to inherit eternal life?”
text.Rotherham: And lo! a certain lawyer, arose, putting him to the test, saying—Teacher! by doing what, shall I inherit, life age-abiding?

text.NRSV: He said to him, “What is written in the law? What do you read there?”
text.Rotherham: And, he, said unto him—In the law, what is written? how dost thou read?

text.NRSV: He answered, “You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, and with all your soul, and with all your strength, and with all your mind; and your neighbor as yourself.”
text.Rotherham: And, he, answering, said—Thou shalt love the Lord thy God, out of all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might, and with all thine intention; and thy neighbour, as thyself?

text.NRSV: And he said to him, “You have given the right answer; do this, and you will live.”
text.Rotherham: And he said unto him—Rightly, hast thou answered: This, do, and thou shalt live.

text.NRSV: But wanting to justify himself, he asked Jesus, “And who is my neighbor?”
text.Rotherham: But, he, wishing to justify himself, said unto Jesus—And, who is, my, neighbour?

text.NRSV: Jesus replied, “A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and fell into the hands of robbers, who stripped him, beat him, and went away, leaving him half dead.
text.Rotherham: And taking up [the question], Jesus said—A certain man, was going down from Jerusalem unto Jericho, and, with robbers, fell in,—who, stripping him, and inflicting, wounds, upon him, departed, leaving him, half dead.

text.NRSV: Now by chance a priest was going down that road; and when he saw him, he passed by on the other side.
text.Rotherham: And, by chance, a certain priest, was coming down by that road, and, seeing him, passed by, on the opposite side.

text.NRSV: So likewise a Levite, when he came to the place and saw him, passed by on the other side.
text.Rotherham: And, in like manner, a Levite also, coming down to the place, and seeing him, passed by, on the opposite side.

text.NRSV: But a Samaritan while traveling came near him; and when he saw him, he was moved with compassion.
text.Rotherham: But, a certain Samaritan, going on his journey, came down to him, and, seeing him, was moved with compassion;

text.NRSV: He went to him and bandaged his wounds, having poured oil and wine on them. Then he put him on his own animal, brought him to an inn, and took care of him.
text.Rotherham: and, coming near, bound up his bruises, pouring thereon oil and wine,—and, setting him on his own beast, brought him into an inn, and took care of him.

text.NRSV: The next day he took out two denarii, gave them to the innkeeper, and said, ‘Take care of him; and when I come back, I will repay you whatever more you spend.’
text.Rotherham: And, on the morrow, throwing out two denaries, he gave them to the inn-keeper, and said—Take care of him, and, whatsoever thou shall further spend, I, when on my way back, will duly pay thee.

text.NRSV: Which of these three, do you think, was a neighbor to the man who fell into the hands of the robbers?”
text.Rotherham: Which of these three, seemeth unto thee to have become, neighbour, unto him who fell among the robbers?

text.NRSV: He said, “The one who showed him mercy.” Jesus said to him, “Go and do likewise.”
text.Rotherham: And, he, said—He who dealt mercifully with him. And Jesus said unto him—Be taking thy journey, and, thou, be doing, in like manner.

text.NRSV: Now as they went on their way, he entered a certain village, where a woman named Martha welcomed him into her home.
text.Rotherham: And, as they were journeying, he, entered into a certain village; and, a certain woman, named Martha, welcomed him into her house.

text.NRSV: She had a sister named Mary, who sat at the Lord’s feet and listened to what he was saying.
text.Rotherham: And, she, had a sister, called Mary, who also, seating herself at the feet of the Lord, was hearing his word.

text.NRSV: But Martha was distracted by her many tasks; so she came to him and asked, “Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to do all the work by myself? Tell her then to help me.”
text.Rotherham: But, Martha, was distracted about much ministering, and, coming near, said—Lord! carest thou not that, my sister, hath left me to be ministering, alone? Speak to her, then, that she help me.

text.NRSV: But the Lord answered her, “Martha, Martha, you are worried and distracted by many things;
text.Rotherham: But the Lord, answering, said to her—Martha! Martha! thou art anxious and troubled about many things:

text.NRSV: there is need of only one thing. Mary has chosen the better part, which will not be taken away from her.”
text.Rotherham: Of few things, is there need, or, of one; Mary, in fact, hath chosen, the good part,—one which shall not be taken away from her.

text.NRSV: He was praying in a certain place, and after he had finished, one of his disciples said to him, “Lord, teach us to pray, as John taught his disciples.”
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when he was in a certain place praying, as he ceased, one of his disciples said unto him—Lord! teach us to pray, as, John also, taught his disciples.

text.NRSV: He said to them, “When you pray, say: Father, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them—Whensoever ye are praying, say—Father! Hallowed be thy name, Come may thy kingdom,

text.NRSV: Give us each day our daily bread.
text.Rotherham: Our needful bread, be giving us, day by day;

text.NRSV: And forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone indebted to us. And do not bring us to the time of trial.”
text.Rotherham: And forgive us our sins, for, even we ourselves, forgive every one indebted to us; And bring us not into temptation.

text.NRSV: And he said to them, “Suppose one of you has a friend, and you go to him at midnight and say to him, ‘Friend, lend me three loaves of bread;
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them—Who from among you, shall have a friend, and shall go unto him at midnight, and say unto him—Friend! supply me with three loaves,

text.NRSV: for a friend of mine has arrived, and I have nothing to set before him.’
text.Rotherham: forasmuch as, a friend of mine, hath come off a journey unto me, and I have not what I can set before him;

text.NRSV: And he answers from within, ‘Do not bother me; the door has already been locked, and my children are with me in bed; I cannot get up and give you anything.’
text.Rotherham: and, he, from within, shall answer, and say—Be not disturbing me,—already, the door hath been fastened, and, my children, with me, are, in bed: I cannot rise and give thee?

text.NRSV: I tell you, even though he will not get up and give him anything because he is his friend, at least because of his persistence he will get up and give him whatever he needs.
text.Rotherham: I say unto you—Even though he will not give him, rising because of his being a friend of his, because, at least, of his importunity, he will rouse himself, and give him as many as he needeth.

text.NRSV: “So I say to you, Ask, and it will be given you; search, and you will find; knock, and the door will be opened for you.
text.Rotherham: I, therefore, unto you, say—Be asking, and it shall be given to you, Be seeking, and ye shall find,—Be knocking, and it shall be opened unto you.

text.NRSV: For everyone who asks receives, and everyone who searches finds, and for everyone who knocks, the door will be opened.
text.Rotherham: For, whosoever asketh, receiveth, and, he that seeketh, findeth,—and, to him that knocketh, shall it be opened.

text.NRSV: Is there anyone among you who, if your child asks for a fish, will give a snake instead of a fish?
text.Rotherham: But which is the father, from among yourselves, whom the son will ask for, a fish, who, instead of a fish, will give him, a serpent?

text.NRSV: Or if the child asks for an egg, will give a scorpion?
text.Rotherham: Or shall also ask, an egg, who will give him, a scorpion?

text.NRSV: If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will the heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask him!”
text.Rotherham: If therefore, ye, being, evil, know how to be giving, good gifts, unto your children, how much rather, will, the heavenly Father, give Holy Spirit unto them that ask him!

text.NRSV: Now he was casting out a demon that was mute; when the demon had gone out, the one who had been mute spoke, and the crowds were amazed.
text.Rotherham: And he was casting out a demon that was dumb; and it came to pass, when the demon went out, the dumb began speaking. And the multitudes marvelled;

text.NRSV: But some of them said, “He casts out demons by Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons.”
text.Rotherham: but, some from among them, said—In Beelzebul, the ruler of the demons, is he casting out the demons;

text.NRSV: Others, to test him, kept demanding from him a sign from heaven.
text.Rotherham: and, others, putting him to the test, a sign out of heaven, were seeking from him.

text.NRSV: But he knew what they were thinking and said to them, “Every kingdom divided against itself becomes a desert, and house falls on house.
text.Rotherham: But, he, knowing their thoughts, said unto them—Every kingdom, against itself divided, is laid waste, and, a house, against a house, [divided], falleth;

text.NRSV: If Satan also is divided against himself, how will his kingdom stand? — for you say that I cast out demons by Beelzebul.
text.Rotherham: And, if, even Satan, against himself hath become divided, how shall his kingdom stand? because ye are saying that, in Beelzebul, am I casting out the demons.

text.NRSV: Now if I cast out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your exorcists cast them out? Therefore they will be your judges.
text.Rotherham: But, if, I, in Beelzebul, am casting out the demons, In whom are, your sons, casting them out? Wherefore, they, shall be, your judges.

text.NRSV: But if it is by the finger of God that I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come to you.
text.Rotherham: But, if, with the finger of God, I, am casting out the demons, then doubtless unawares hath come upon you, the kingdom of God.

text.NRSV: When a strong man, fully armed, guards his castle, his property is safe.
text.Rotherham: Whensoever, the mighty one, armed, may be guarding his own dwelling, in peace, are his goods;

text.NRSV: But when one stronger than he attacks him and overpowers him, he takes away his armor in which he trusted and divides his plunder.
text.Rotherham: But, whensoever, a mightier than he, shall come upon and vanquish him, his panoply, he taketh away, wherein he was trusting, and, his spoils, he distributeth.

text.NRSV: Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters.
text.Rotherham: He that is not with me, is, against me; and, he that gathereth not with me, scattereth.

text.NRSV: “When the unclean spirit has gone out of a person, it wanders through waterless regions looking for a resting place, but not finding any, it says, ‘I will return to my house from which I came.’
text.Rotherham: Whensoever, the impure spirit, goeth out from the man, it passeth through waterless places, seeking rest; and, not finding it, [then,] it saith, I will return unto my house, whence I came out;—

text.NRSV: When it comes, it finds it swept and put in order.
text.Rotherham: and, coming, findeth it, [empty,] swept, and adorned.

text.NRSV: Then it goes and brings seven other spirits more evil than itself, and they enter and live there; and the last state of that person is worse than the first.”
text.Rotherham: Then, goeth it, and taketh along with itself, other spirits, more wicked than itself—seven, and, entering in, fixeth its dwelling there; and, the last state of that man, becometh, worse than the first.

text.NRSV: While he was saying this, a woman in the crowd raised her voice and said to him, “Blessed is the womb that bore you and the breasts that nursed you!”
text.Rotherham: Now it came to pass, while he was saying these things, that a certain woman out of the multitude, lifting up her voice, said unto him—Happy the womb that bare thee! And the breasts which thou didst suck!

text.NRSV: But he said, “Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and obey it!”
text.Rotherham: But, he, said—Yea rather!—Happy they who hear the word of God, and observe it!

text.NRSV: When the crowds were increasing, he began to say, “This generation is an evil generation; it asks for a sign, but no sign will be given to it except the sign of Jonah.
text.Rotherham: And, as the multitudes were thronging together, he began to be saying—This generation, is, a wicked generation: A sign, it is seeking, and, a sign, shall not be given it, save the sign of Jonah.

text.NRSV: For just as Jonah became a sign to the people of Nineveh, so the Son of Man will be to this generation.
text.Rotherham: For, according as, Jonah, became, unto the Ninevites, a sign, so, shall be, the Son of Man also, unto this generation.

text.NRSV: The queen of the South will rise at the judgment with the people of this generation and condemn them, because she came from the ends of the earth to listen to the wisdom of Solomon, and see, something greater than Solomon is here!
text.Rotherham: The queen of the south, will rise up, in the judgment, with the men of this generation, and will condemn them; because she came out of the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon,—and lo! something more than Solomon, here.

text.NRSV: The people of Nineveh will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, because they repented at the proclamation of Jonah, and see, something greater than Jonah is here!
text.Rotherham: Men of Nineveh, will rise up, in the judgment, with this generation, and will condemn it; because they repented into the proclamation of Jonah,—and lo! something more than Jonah, here.

text.NRSV: “No one after lighting a lamp puts it in a cellar or under a bushel basket, but on a lampstand, so that those who enter may see the light.
text.Rotherham: No one, having lighted, a lamp, into a covered place, a putteth it, nor, under the measure; but upon the lampstand, that they who enter may see, the light.

text.NRSV: Your eye is the lamp of your body. If your eye is healthy, your whole body is full of light; but if it is not healthy, your body is full of darkness.
text.Rotherham: The lamp of thy body, is thine eye: Whensoever, thine eye, may be, single, even the whole of thy body, is, lighted up; but, whensoever it may be, useless, even thy body, is darkened.

text.NRSV: Therefore consider whether the light in you is not darkness.
text.Rotherham: Be looking to it, therefore, lest, the light that is in thee, be, darkness.

text.NRSV: If then your whole body is full of light, with no part of it in darkness, it will be as full of light as when a lamp gives you light with its rays.”
text.Rotherham: If, therefore, thy whole body, is lighted up, not having any part darkened, the whole, shall be lighted up, as whensoever, the lamp, with its radiance, may be giving thee light.

text.NRSV: While he was speaking, a Pharisee invited him to dine with him; so he went in and took his place at the table.
text.Rotherham: And, when he had spoken, a Pharisee was requesting him that he would dine with him; and, entering, he reclined.

text.NRSV: The Pharisee was amazed to see that he did not first wash before dinner.
text.Rotherham: And, the Pharisee, beholding, marvelled that he was not, first, immersed, before the dinner.

text.NRSV: Then the Lord said to him, “Now you Pharisees clean the outside of the cup and of the dish, but inside you are full of greed and wickedness.
text.Rotherham: And the Lord said unto him: Now, ye, the Pharisees, the outside of the cup and of the tray, do make pure; but, your inward part, is full of plunder and wickedness.

text.NRSV: You fools! Did not the one who made the outside make the inside also?
text.Rotherham: Simple ones! Did not, he who made the outside, the inside also, make?

text.NRSV: So give for alms those things that are within; and see, everything will be clean for you.
text.Rotherham: Notwithstanding, as to the things within, give alms, and lo! everything, is, pure unto you.

text.NRSV: “But woe to you Pharisees! For you tithe mint and rue and herbs of all kinds, and neglect justice and the love of God; it is these you ought to have practiced, without neglecting the others.
text.Rotherham: But alas for you, the Pharisees! because ye tithe the mint, and the rue, and every garden herb, and pass by justice, and the love of God. But, these things, it was binding to do, and, those, not to pass by.

text.NRSV: Woe to you Pharisees! For you love to have the seat of honor in the synagogues and to be greeted with respect in the marketplaces.
text.Rotherham: Alas for you, the Pharisees! because ye love the first seats in the synagogues, and the salutations in the market-places!

text.NRSV: Woe to you! For you are like unmarked graves, and people walk over them without knowing it.”
text.Rotherham: Alas for you! because ye are as the secret tombs: even the men that are walking above them, know it not.

text.NRSV: One of the lawyers answered him, “Teacher, when you say these things, you insult us too.”
text.Rotherham: And, making answer, one of the Lawyers saith unto him, Teacher! these things, saying, us also, dost thou insult!

text.NRSV: And he said, “Woe also to you lawyers! For you load people with burdens hard to bear, and you yourselves do not lift a finger to ease them.
text.Rotherham: And, he, said—And, for you, the lawyers, alas! because ye lade men with burdens hard to be borne, and, yourselves, with one of your fingers, touch not the burdens.

text.NRSV: Woe to you! For you build the tombs of the prophets whom your ancestors killed.
text.Rotherham: Alas for you! because ye build the tombs of the prophets, and your fathers slew them!

text.NRSV: So you are witnesses and approve of the deeds of your ancestors; for they killed them, and you build their tombs.
text.Rotherham: Hence, witnesses, are ye, and find consenting pleasure in the works of your fathers: because, they, indeed, slew them, and, ye, are building, [their tombs] .

text.NRSV: Therefore also the Wisdom of God said, ‘I will send them prophets and apostles, some of whom they will kill and persecute,’
text.Rotherham: For this cause, the Wisdom of God hath said—I will send forth, unto them, prophets and apostles,—and, some from among them, will they slay, and, [some] persecute:

text.NRSV: so that this generation may be charged with the blood of all the prophets shed since the foundation of the world,
text.Rotherham: That the blood of all the prophets which hath been shed from the foundation of the world, may be sought out, from this generation,—

text.NRSV: from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who perished between the altar and the sanctuary. Yes, I tell you, it will be charged against this generation.
text.Rotherham: From the blood of Abel, unto the blood of Zachariah who was destroyed betwixt the altar and the house; Yea! I say unto you—It shall be sought out from this generation.

text.NRSV: Woe to you lawyers! For you have taken away the key of knowledge; you did not enter yourselves, and you hindered those who were entering.”
text.Rotherham: Alas for you, the lawyers! because ye took away the key of knowledge: yourselves, entered not, and, them who were entering, ye hindered.

text.NRSV: When he went outside, the scribes and the Pharisees began to be very hostile toward him and to cross-examine him about many things,
text.Rotherham: And, when, from thence, he came out, the Scribes and the Pharisees began, with vehemence, to be hemming him in, and trying to make him speak off-hand concerning many things,—

text.NRSV: lying in wait for him, to catch him in something he might say.
text.Rotherham: lying in wait for him, to catch something out of his mouth.

text.NRSV: Meanwhile, when the crowd gathered by the thousands, so that they trampled on one another, he began to speak first to his disciples, “Beware of the yeast of the Pharisees, that is, their hypocrisy.
text.Rotherham: Amongst which things, when the ten thousands of the multitude were gathered together, so that they were treading one upon another, he began to be saying, unto his disciples, first—Be keeping yourselves free from the leaven of the Pharisees, the which is, hypocrisy.

text.NRSV: Nothing is covered up that will not be uncovered, and nothing secret that will not become known.
text.Rotherham: But, nothing, hath been, covered up, which shall not be uncovered, and hidden, which shall not be made known.

text.NRSV: Therefore whatever you have said in the dark will be heard in the light, and what you have whispered in private rooms will be proclaimed from the housetops.
text.Rotherham: Because, as many things as, in the darkness, ye have said, in the light, shall be heard; and, what to the ear ye spake, in the chambers, shall be proclaimed on the housetops.

text.NRSV: “I tell you, my friends, do not fear those who kill the body, and after that can do nothing more.
text.Rotherham: And I say unto you, my friends—Do not be put in fear of them who kill the body, and, after these things, have nothing more uncommon which they can do.

text.NRSV: But I will warn you whom to fear: fear him who, after he has killed, has authority to cast into hell. Yes, I tell you, fear him!
text.Rotherham: But I will suggest to you, whom ye should fear—Fear him who, after killing, hath authority to cast into gehenna,—Yea, I say unto you—Him, fear ye.

text.NRSV: Are not five sparrows sold for two pennies? Yet not one of them is forgotten in God’s sight.
text.Rotherham: Are not, five sparrows, sold for two farthings? and, not one from among them, hath been forgotten before God.

text.NRSV: But even the hairs of your head are all counted. Do not be afraid; you are of more value than many sparrows.
text.Rotherham: But, even the hairs of your head, have all been numbered: Be not afraid: many sparrows, ye excel.

text.NRSV: “And I tell you, everyone who acknowledges me before others, the Son of Man also will acknowledge before the angels of God;
text.Rotherham: And I say unto you—Whosoever shall confess me before men, even the Son of Man, will confess him, before the messengers of God;

text.NRSV: but whoever denies me before others will be denied before the angels of God.
text.Rotherham: But, he who denied me before men, shall be denied before the messengers of God.

text.NRSV: And everyone who speaks a word against the Son of Man will be forgiven; but whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit will not be forgiven.
text.Rotherham: And, whosoever shall say a word against the Son of Man, it shall be forgiven him; but, unto him who, against the Holy Spirit, speaketh profanely, it shall not be forgiven.

text.NRSV: When they bring you before the synagogues, the rulers, and the authorities, do not worry about how you are to defend yourselves or what you are to say;
text.Rotherham: But, whensoever they shall be bringing you in before the synagogues, and the rulers, and the authorities, do not be anxious how, [or what] ye shall answer, or what ye shall say;

text.NRSV: for the Holy Spirit will teach you at that very hour what you ought to say.”
text.Rotherham: For, the Holy Spirit, shall teach you, in that very hour, what ye ought to say.

text.NRSV: Someone in the crowd said to him, “Teacher, tell my brother to divide the family inheritance with me.”
text.Rotherham: And one from amongst the multitude said unto him—Teacher! bid my brother divide with me the inheritance.

text.NRSV: But he said to him, “Friend, who set me to be a judge or arbitrator over you?”
text.Rotherham: But, he, said unto him—Man! who hath appointed me a judge or divider over you?

text.NRSV: And he said to them, “Take care! Be on your guard against all kinds of greed; for one’s life does not consist in the abundance of possessions.”
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them—Mind and be guarding yourselves from all covetousness; for not, in one’s abundance, doth his life spring out of his possessions.

text.NRSV: Then he told them a parable: “The land of a rich man produced abundantly.
text.Rotherham: And he spake a parable unto them, saying—A certain rich man’s estate, bare well.

text.NRSV: And he thought to himself, ‘What should I do, for I have no place to store my crops?’
text.Rotherham: And he began to deliberate within himself, saying—What shall I do? because I have not where I can gather my fruits.

text.NRSV: Then he said, ‘I will do this: I will pull down my barns and build larger ones, and there I will store all my grain and my goods.
text.Rotherham: And he said—This, will I do,—I will pull down my barns, and, greater ones, build, and gather, there, all my wheat and good things;

text.NRSV: And I will say to my soul, Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years; relax, eat, drink, be merry.’
text.Rotherham: and will say to my soul—Soul! thou hast many good things [lying by for many years: be taking thy rest, eat, drink,] be making merry!

text.NRSV: But God said to him, ‘You fool! This very night your life is being demanded of you. And the things you have prepared, whose will they be?’
text.Rotherham: But God said unto him—Simple one! on this very night, they are asking, thy soul, from thee; The things, then, which thou hast prepared, whose shall they be?

text.NRSV: So it is with those who store up treasures for themselves but are not rich toward God.”
text.Rotherham: [So, is he that is laying up treasure for himself, and is not rich, towards God.]

text.NRSV: He said to his disciples, “Therefore I tell you, do not worry about your life, what you will eat, or about your body, what you will wear.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto his disciples—For this cause, I say unto you,—Be not anxious for the life, what ye shall eat, nor yet for [your] body, what ye shall put on;

text.NRSV: For life is more than food, and the body more than clothing.
text.Rotherham: For, the life, is more, than the food, and, the body, than, the clothing.

text.NRSV: Consider the ravens: they neither sow nor reap, they have neither storehouse nor barn, and yet God feeds them. Of how much more value are you than the birds!
text.Rotherham: Consider well the ravens—That they sow nor, neither do they reap, which have neither chamber nor barn,—and, God, feedeth, them! By how much do, ye, excel, the birds?

text.NRSV: And can any of you by worrying add a single hour to your span of life?
text.Rotherham: And, who from among you, though anxious, can, unto his stature, add a cubit?

text.NRSV: If then you are not able to do so small a thing as that, why do you worry about the rest?
text.Rotherham: If then, not even the least thing, ye can do, why, concerning the rest, are ye anxious?

text.NRSV: Consider the lilies, how they grow: they neither toil nor spin; yet I tell you, even Solomon in all his glory was not clothed like one of these.
text.Rotherham: Consider well the lilies, how they grow: they toil not, neither do they spin, and yet I say unto you—Not even Solomon, in all his glory, was arrayed like one of these.

text.NRSV: But if God so clothes the grass of the field, which is alive today and tomorrow is thrown into the oven, how much more will he clothe you — you of little faith!
text.Rotherham: But, if God thus adorneth, the grass, which is, in a field, to-day, and, to-morrow, into an oven, is cast, how much rather you, O little-of-faith?

text.NRSV: And do not keep striving for what you are to eat and what you are to drink, and do not keep worrying.
text.Rotherham: Ye, therefore, be not seeking what ye shall eat and what ye shall drink, and be not held in suspense;

text.NRSV: For it is the nations of the world that strive after all these things, and your Father knows that you need them.
text.Rotherham: For, after all these things, do, the nations of the world, seek,—but, your Father, knoweth that ye need these things.

text.NRSV: Instead, strive for his kingdom, and these things will be given to you as well.
text.Rotherham: Notwithstanding, be seeking his kingdom,—and, these things, shall be added unto you.

text.NRSV: “Do not be afraid, little flock, for it is your Father’s good pleasure to give you the kingdom.
text.Rotherham: Be not afraid, the dear little flock! for your Father delighteth to give you, the kingdom.

text.NRSV: Sell your possessions, and give alms. Make purses for yourselves that do not wear out, an unfailing treasure in heaven, where no thief comes near and no moth destroys.
text.Rotherham: Sell your possessions, and give alms, make for yourselves purses that wax not old,—treasure unfailing, in the heavens, where, thief, doth not draw near, and, moth, doth not spoil.

text.NRSV: For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.
text.Rotherham: For, where your treasure is, there, will your heart he also.

text.NRSV: “Be dressed for action and have your lamps lit;
text.Rotherham: Let your loins be girded, and your lamps burning,

text.NRSV: be like those who are waiting for their master to return from the wedding banquet, so that they may open the door for him as soon as he comes and knocks.
text.Rotherham: And, ye yourselves, like unto men awaiting their own lord, once he may break up out of the marriage-feast,—that, when he cometh and knocketh, straightway, they may open unto him.

text.NRSV: Blessed are those slaves whom the master finds alert when he comes; truly I tell you, he will fasten his belt and have them sit down to eat, and he will come and serve them.
text.Rotherham: Happy those servants, whom the lord, when he cometh, shall find watching! Verily, I say unto you—he will gird himself, and make them recline, and, coming near, will minister unto them.

text.NRSV: If he comes during the middle of the night, or near dawn, and finds them so, blessed are those slaves.
text.Rotherham: And, if, in the second, or if, in the third, watch, he come and find, thus, happy, are, they!

text.NRSV: “But know this: if the owner of the house had known at what hour the thief was coming, he would not have let his house be broken into.
text.Rotherham: But, of this, be taking note—Had the householder known, in what hour the thief was coming, he would have watched, and not suffered his house, to be digged through.

text.NRSV: You also must be ready, for the Son of Man is coming at an unexpected hour.”
text.Rotherham: Ye, therefore, be getting ready, because, in what hour ye are not thinking, The Son of Man cometh!

text.NRSV: Peter said, “Lord, are you telling this parable for us or for everyone?”
text.Rotherham: But Peter said—Lord! unto us, this parable, speakest thou? or, even unto all?

text.NRSV: And the Lord said, “Who then is the faithful and prudent manager whom his master will put in charge of his slaves, to give them their allowance of food at the proper time?
text.Rotherham: And the Lord said—Who then is the faithful steward, the prudent one, whom the lord will appoint over his body of attendants, to be giving, in due season, the measured allowance of wheat?

text.NRSV: Blessed is that slave whom his master will find at work when he arrives.
text.Rotherham: Happy, that servant, whom the lord, when he cometh shall find doing, thus!

text.NRSV: Truly I tell you, he will put that one in charge of all his possessions.
text.Rotherham: Of a truth, I say unto you—Over all his possessions, will he appoint him.

text.NRSV: But if that slave says to himself, ‘My master is delayed in coming,’ and if he begins to beat the other slaves, men and women, and to eat and drink and get drunk,
text.Rotherham: But, if that servant should say in his heart—My lord delayeth to come! and should begin to be striking the youths and the maidens,—to be eating also, and drinking, and making himself drunk,

text.NRSV: the master of that slave will come on a day when he does not expect him and at an hour that he does not know, and will cut him in pieces, and put him with the unfaithful.
text.Rotherham: The lord of that servant, will have come—on a day when he is not expecting, and in an hour when he is not taking note,—and will cut him asunder, and, his part, with the unfaithful, will appoint.

text.NRSV: That slave who knew what his master wanted, but did not prepare himself or do what was wanted, will receive a severe beating.
text.Rotherham: And, that servant, who had come to know the will of his lord, and neither prepared, nor wrought unto his will, shall be beaten with many stripes;

text.NRSV: But the one who did not know and did what deserved a beating will receive a light beating. From everyone to whom much has been given, much will be required; and from the one to whom much has been entrusted, even more will be demanded.
text.Rotherham: Whereas, he who had not come to know, and did things worthy of stripes, shall be beaten with few stripes. And, every one to whom was given much, much, shall be sought from him; and, he to whom they committed much, for more than common, will they ask him.

text.NRSV: “I came to bring fire to the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!
text.Rotherham: Fire, came I to cast upon the earth,—and what can I wish, if, already, it hath been, kindled?

text.NRSV: I have a baptism with which to be baptized, and what stress I am under until it is completed!
text.Rotherham: But, an immersion, have I, to be immersed with, and how am I distressed, until it be ended!

text.NRSV: Do you think that I have come to bring peace to the earth? No, I tell you, but rather division!
text.Rotherham: Suppose ye, that, peace, I am come to give in the earth? Nay, I tell you, but rather division.

text.NRSV: From now on five in one household will be divided, three against two and two against three;
text.Rotherham: For there shall be, henceforth, five in one house, divided,—three against two, and two against three:

text.NRSV: they will be divided: father against son and son against father, mother against daughter and daughter against mother, mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law and daughter-in-law against mother-in-law.”
text.Rotherham: There shall be divided—Father against son, and son against father, mother against daughter, and daughter against the mother,—mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law, and daughter-in-law against the mother-in-law.

text.NRSV: He also said to the crowds, “When you see a cloud rising in the west, you immediately say, ‘It is going to rain’; and so it happens.
text.Rotherham: And he went on to say, even unto the multitudes,—Whensoever ye see a cloud springing up from the west, straightway, ye are saying—A thunderstorm is coming! and it happeneth thus.

text.NRSV: And when you see the south wind blowing, you say, ‘There will be scorching heat’; and it happens.
text.Rotherham: And, whensoever a south wind, blowing, ye say—A scorching heat, will there be! and it cometh to pass.

text.NRSV: You hypocrites! You know how to interpret the appearance of earth and sky, but why do you not know how to interpret the present time?
text.Rotherham: Hypocrites! the face of the earth and of the heaven, ye know how to scan; but, this season, how know ye not to scan;

text.NRSV: “And why do you not judge for yourselves what is right?
text.Rotherham: Why, moreover, even from yourselves, judge ye not what is just?

text.NRSV: Thus, when you go with your accuser before a magistrate, on the way make an effort to settle the case, or you may be dragged before the judge, and the judge hand you over to the officer, and the officer throw you into prison.
text.Rotherham: For, as thou art going along with thine adversary unto a ruler, on the way, take pains to get a release from him; lest once he drag thee along unto the judge, and, the judge, deliver thee up to the punisher,—and, the punisher, cast thee into prison:

text.NRSV: I tell you, you will never get out until you have paid the very last penny.”
text.Rotherham: I tell thee—In nowise shalt thou come out from thence, until, even the last fraction, thou pay!

text.NRSV: At that very time there were some present who told him about the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
text.Rotherham: Now there were present some, in that very season, bringing tidings to him, concerning the Galilaeans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.

text.NRSV: He asked them, “Do you think that because these Galileans suffered in this way they were worse sinners than all other Galileans?
text.Rotherham: And, answering, he said unto them—Suppose ye, that, these Galilaeans, had become, sinners beyond all the Galilaeans, because, these things, they have suffered?

text.NRSV: No, I tell you; but unless you repent, you will all perish as they did.
text.Rotherham: Nay! I tell you, but, except ye repent, ye, all, in like manner, shall perish.

text.NRSV: Or those eighteen who were killed when the tower of Siloam fell on them—do you think that they were worse offenders than all the others living in Jerusalem?
text.Rotherham: Or, those eighteen, upon whom fell the tower in Siloam, and slew them, suppose ye that, they, had become, debtors, beyond all the men who were dwelling in Jerusalem?

text.NRSV: No, I tell you; but unless you repent, you will all perish as they did.”
text.Rotherham: Nay! I tell you, but, except ye repent, ye, all, in the same way, shall perish.

text.NRSV: Then he told this parable: “A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard; and he came looking for fruit on it and found none.
text.Rotherham: And he went on to speak this parable:—A certain man had, a fig-tree, planted in his vineyard, and he canto seeking fruit therein, and found none.

text.NRSV: So he said to the gardener, ‘See here! For three years I have come looking for fruit on this fig tree, and still I find none. Cut it down! Why should it be wasting the soil?’
text.Rotherham: And he said unto the vine-dresser—Lo! three years, I come, seeking fruit in this fig-tree, and find none. Cut it down! Why doth it make, even the round useless?

text.NRSV: He replied, ‘Sir, let it alone for one more year, until I dig around it and put manure on it.
text.Rotherham: And, he, answering, saith unto him—Sir! let it alone this year also, until such time as I dig about it, and throw in manure,—

text.NRSV: If it bears fruit next year, well and good; but if not, you can cut it down.’”
text.Rotherham: and if it may bear fruit for the future; …but, otherwise, certainly, thou shalt cut it down.

text.NRSV: Now he was teaching in one of the synagogues on the sabbath.
text.Rotherham: And he was teaching in one of the synagogues, on the Sabbath.

text.NRSV: And just then there appeared a woman with a spirit that had crippled her for eighteen years. She was bent over and was quite unable to stand up straight.
text.Rotherham: And lo! a woman, having a spirit of weakness eighteen years,—and was bowed together, and unable to lift herself up, at all.

text.NRSV: When Jesus saw her, he called her over and said, “Woman, you are set free from your ailment.”
text.Rotherham: And, seeing her, Jesus called her and said to her—Woman! thou art loosed from thy weakness,—

text.NRSV: When he laid his hands on her, immediately she stood up straight and began praising God.
text.Rotherham: and laid on her his hands; and, instantly, she was made straight again, and began glorifying God.

text.NRSV: But the leader of the synagogue, indignant because Jesus had cured on the sabbath, kept saying to the crowd, “There are six days on which work ought to be done; come on those days and be cured, and not on the sabbath day.”
text.Rotherham: But the synagogue-ruler, answering, being greatly displeased that, on the Sabbath, Jesus had healed, began saying unto the multitude—Six days, there are, in which men ought to get their work done; On them, therefore, come and be healed, and, not on the day of rest.

text.NRSV: But the Lord answered him and said, “You hypocrites! Does not each of you on the sabbath untie his ox or his donkey from the manger, and lead it away to give it water?
text.Rotherham: The Lord answered him, and said—Hypocrites! Doth not, each one of you, on the Sabbath, loose his ox or ass from the manger, and, leading it away, give it drink?

text.NRSV: And ought not this woman, a daughter of Abraham whom Satan bound for eighteen long years, be set free from this bondage on the sabbath day?”
text.Rotherham: But, this woman, being a daughter of Abraham, whom Satan had bound, lo! eighteen years, was there not a needs-be that she should be loosed from this bond, on the day of rest?

text.NRSV: When he said this, all his opponents were put to shame; and the entire crowd was rejoicing at all the wonderful things that he was doing.
text.Rotherham: And, as he was saying, these, things, all who had been setting themselves against him were being put to shame; and, all the multitude, were rejoicing over all the glorious things which were being brought to pass by him.

text.NRSV: He said therefore, “What is the kingdom of God like? And to what should I compare it?
text.Rotherham: He went on to say, therefore—Whereunto, is the kingdom of God, like? And, whereunto, shall I liken it?

text.NRSV: It is like a mustard seed that someone took and sowed in the garden; it grew and became a tree, and the birds of the air made nests in its branches.”
text.Rotherham: It is, like, unto a grain of mustard seed, which a man took and cast into his own garden; and it grew, and became a tree, and, the birds of heaven, lodged amongst its branches.

text.NRSV: And again he said, “To what should I compare the kingdom of God?
text.Rotherham: And, again, he said—Whereunto, shall I liken the kingdom of God?

text.NRSV: It is like yeast that a woman took and mixed in with three measures of flour until all of it was leavened.”
text.Rotherham: It is, like leaven, which a woman took and hid in three measures of flour, until, the whole, was leavened.

text.NRSV: Jesus went through one town and village after another, teaching as he made his way to Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: And he was journeying on, city by city and village by village, and making, progress, unto Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Someone asked him, “Lord, will only a few be saved?” He said to them,
text.Rotherham: And one said unto him—Lord! are they, few, who are being saved? And, he, said unto them—

text.NRSV: “Strive to enter through the narrow door; for many, I tell you, will try to enter and will not be able.
text.Rotherham: Be striving to enter through the narrow door; for, many, I say unto you, will seek to enter, and will not be able,—

text.NRSV: When once the owner of the house has got up and shut the door, and you begin to stand outside and to knock at the door, saying, ‘Lord, open to us,’ then in reply he will say to you, ‘I do not know where you come from.’
text.Rotherham: when once the householder shall rouse himself, and lock the door, and ye begin to stand, outside, and to knock at the door, saying—Lord! open to us; and he shall answer and say unto you—I know you not, whence ye are.

text.NRSV: Then you will begin to say, ‘We ate and drank with you, and you taught in our streets.’
text.Rotherham: Then, will ye begin to say—We did eat and drink in thy presence, and, in our broadways, thou didst, teach;

text.NRSV: But he will say, ‘I do not know where you come from; go away from me, all you evildoers!’
text.Rotherham: And he will speak, saying unto you—I know not whence ye are; Depart from me, all workers of unrighteous—

text.NRSV: There will be weeping and gnashing of teeth when you see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets in the kingdom of God, and you yourselves thrown out.
text.Rotherham: There, will be weeping and gnashing of teeth, as soon as ye see Abraham and Isaac and Jacob and all the prophets, in the kingdom of God, and, yourselves, being thrust forth outside.

text.NRSV: Then people will come from east and west, from north and south, and will eat in the kingdom of God.
text.Rotherham: And they shall have come from east and west, and from north and south, and be made recline in the kingdom of God.

text.NRSV: Indeed, some are last who will be first, and some are first who will be last.”
text.Rotherham: And lo! there are, last, who shall be, first, and there are, first, who shall be, last;

text.NRSV: At that very hour some Pharisees came and said to him, “Get away from here, for Herod wants to kill you.”
text.Rotherham: In that very hour, came near certain Pharisees, saying unto him, Go forth, and be journeying hence, because, Herod, desireth to slay thee.

text.NRSV: He said to them, “Go and tell that fox for me, ‘Listen, I am casting out demons and performing cures today and tomorrow, and on the third day I finish my work.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto them—Go and tell this fox, Lo! I am casting out demons, and, cures, am I finishing, to-day, and to-morrow,—

text.NRSV: Yet today, tomorrow, and the next day I must be on my way, because it is impossible for a prophet to be killed outside of Jerusalem.’
text.Rotherham: and, on the third, I am to be made perfect. Nevertheless, I must needs, to-day, and tomorrow, and the following, be journeying on, because it is impossible that a prophet perish, outside Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often have I desired to gather your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!
text.Rotherham: Jerusalem! Jerusalem! she that slayeth the prophets, and stoneth them that are sent unto her! How often, have I desired to gather together thy children, like as a hen, her own brood, under her wings,—and ye did not desire!

text.NRSV: See, your house is left to you. And I tell you, you will not see me until the time comes when you say, ‘Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord.’”
text.Rotherham: Lo! your house is left to you. [And] I say unto you—In nowise shall ye see me, until ye say,—Blessed, is he that cometh in the name of the Lord!

text.NRSV: On one occasion when Jesus was going to the house of a leader of the Pharisees to eat a meal on the sabbath, they were watching him closely.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, when he entered into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees, on a Sabbath, to eat bread, that, they, were narrowly watching him.

text.NRSV: Just then, in front of him, there was a man who had dropsy.
text.Rotherham: And lo! there was, a certain man, who had the dropsy, before him.

text.NRSV: And Jesus asked the lawyers and Pharisees, “Is it lawful to cure people on the sabbath, or not?”
text.Rotherham: And Jesus, answering, spake unto the Lawyers and Pharisees, saying—Is it allowed, on the Sabbath, to cure, or not? But, they, held their peace.

text.NRSV: But they were silent. So Jesus took him and healed him, and sent him away.
text.Rotherham: And taking hold [of him] he healed and dismissed him,—

text.NRSV: Then he said to them, “If one of you has a child or an ox that has fallen into a well, will you not immediately pull it out on a sabbath day?”
text.Rotherham: and, unto them, said—Which of you, shall have a son or an ox that, into a pit, shall fall, and will not straightway pull him up, on the day of rest?

text.NRSV: And they could not reply to this.
text.Rotherham: And they could not return an answer unto these things.

text.NRSV: When he noticed how the guests chose the places of honor, he told them a parable.
text.Rotherham: And he went on to speak, unto the invited, a parable,—observing how, the first couches, they were choosing; saying unto them—

text.NRSV: “When you are invited by someone to a wedding banquet, do not sit down at the place of honor, in case someone more distinguished than you has been invited by your host;
text.Rotherham: Whensoever thou hast been invited by anyone unto a marriage feast, do not recline on the first couch; lest once, a more honourable than thou, have been invited by him,

text.NRSV: and the host who invited both of you may come and say to you, ‘Give this person your place,’ and then in disgrace you would start to take the lowest place.
text.Rotherham: and he that invited both thee and him should come, and say unto thee—Give, unto this one, place! and, then, thou shouldst begin, with shame, the last place, to occupy.

text.NRSV: But when you are invited, go and sit down at the lowest place, so that when your host comes, he may say to you, ‘Friend, move up higher’; then you will be honored in the presence of all who sit at the table with you.
text.Rotherham: But, whensoever thou hast been invited, pass on and fall back into the last place, that, whensoever he that hath invited thee shall come, he may say unto thee—Friend! come close up, higher. Then, shalt thou have honour before all who are reclining together with thee.

text.NRSV: For all who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted.”
text.Rotherham: Because, everyone who exalteth himself, shall be abased; and, he that abaseth himself, shall be exalted?

text.NRSV: He said also to the one who had invited him, “When you give a luncheon or a dinner, do not invite your friends or your brothers or your relatives or rich neighbors, in case they may invite you in return, and you would be repaid.
text.Rotherham: Moreover he went on to say, unto him also who had invited him,—Whensoever thou mayest be making a dinner or a supper, do not call thy friends, or thy brothers, or thy kinsfolk, or rich neighbours,—lest once, they also, invite thee in return, and it become a recompense unto thee.

text.NRSV: But when you give a banquet, invite the poor, the crippled, the lame, and the blind.
text.Rotherham: But, whensoever, an entertainment, thou mayest be making, invite the destitute, the tried, the lame, the blind;

text.NRSV: And you will be blessed, because they cannot repay you, for you will be repaid at the resurrection of the righteous.”
text.Rotherham: and, happy, shalt thou be, that they have not wherewith to recompense thee, for it shall be recompensed unto thee, in the resurrection of the righteous.

text.NRSV: One of the dinner guests, on hearing this, said to him, “Blessed is anyone who will eat bread in the kingdom of God!”
text.Rotherham: And one of those reclining together, hearing these things, said unto him—Happy, whoever shall eat bread in the kingdom of God!

text.NRSV: Then Jesus said to him, “Someone gave a great dinner and invited many.
text.Rotherham: But, he, said unto him—A certain man, was making a great supper, and invited many;

text.NRSV: At the time for the dinner he sent his slave to say to those who had been invited, ‘Come; for everything is ready now.’
text.Rotherham: and he sent out his servant, at the hour of the supper, to say unto the invited—Be coming! because, even now, is it, ready.

text.NRSV: But they all alike began to make excuses. The first said to him, ‘I have bought a piece of land, and I must go out and see it; please accept my apologies.’
text.Rotherham: And they all began, one after another to excuse themselves. The first, said unto him—A field, have I bought, and have need to go out and see it: I request thee, hold me excused.

text.NRSV: Another said, ‘I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to try them out; please accept my apologies.’
text.Rotherham: And, another, said—Five yoke of oxen, have I bought, and am going my way to prove them; I request thee, hold me excused.

text.NRSV: Another said, ‘I have just been married, and therefore I cannot come.’
text.Rotherham: And, another, said—A wife, have I married, and, for this cause, I cannot come!

text.NRSV: So the slave returned and reported this to his master. Then the owner of the house became angry and said to his slave, ‘Go out at once into the streets and lanes of the town and bring in the poor, the crippled, the blind, and the lame.’
text.Rotherham: And, going near, the servant reported unto his lord these things. Then, provoked to anger, the master of the house said unto his servant—Go out quickly, into the broadways and streets of the city,—and, the destitute, and tried, and blind, and lame, bring thou in here.

text.NRSV: And the slave said, ‘Sir, what you ordered has been done, and there is still room.’
text.Rotherham: And the servant said—Lord! what thou didst order, hath been done; and, yet, there is, room.

text.NRSV: Then the master said to the slave, ‘Go out into the roads and lanes, and compel people to come in, so that my house may be filled.
text.Rotherham: And the lord said unto the servant—Go out among the highways and fences, and compel them to come in; that my house may be filled.

text.NRSV: For I tell you, none of those who were invited will taste my dinner.’”
text.Rotherham: For, I say unto you—Not one of those men who had been invited, shall taste of my supper.

text.NRSV: Now large crowds were traveling with him; and he turned and said to them,
text.Rotherham: And there were journeying together with him many multitudes; and, turning, he said unto them—

text.NRSV: “Whoever comes to me and does not hate father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and even life itself, cannot be my disciple.
text.Rotherham: If anyone cometh unto me, and hateth not his own father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brothers, and sisters, further also, even his own life, he cannot be my disciple.

text.NRSV: Whoever does not carry the cross and follow me cannot be my disciple.
text.Rotherham: Whoever beareth not his own cross, and cometh after me, cannot be my disciple.

text.NRSV: For which of you, intending to build a tower, does not first sit down and estimate the cost, to see whether he has enough to complete it?
text.Rotherham: For, who from among you, wishing to build a tower, doth not first sit down and count the cost,—whether he hath sufficient for completion;

text.NRSV: Otherwise, when he has laid a foundation and is not able to finish, all who see it will begin to ridicule him,
text.Rotherham: lest once, he having laid a foundation, and not being able to finish, all who are looking on, should begin to mock at him,

text.NRSV: saying, ‘This person began to build and was not able to finish.’
text.Rotherham: saying—This man, began to build, and was not able to finish!

text.NRSV: Or what king, going out to wage war against another king, will not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to oppose the one who comes against him with twenty thousand?
text.Rotherham: Or, what king, moving on to encounter, another king, in battle, will not sit down first and take counsel, whether he is able, with ten thousand, to meet him who, with twenty thousand, is coming against him.

text.NRSV: If he cannot, then, while the other is still far away, he sends a delegation and asks for the terms of peace.
text.Rotherham: And, if not, by any means, while he is yet afar off, he sendeth, an embassy, and requesteth the conditions of peace.

text.NRSV: So therefore, none of you can become my disciple if you do not give up all your possessions.
text.Rotherham: Thus, therefore, everyone from amongst you, who doth not bid adieu unto all his own possessions, cannot be my disciple.

text.NRSV: “Salt is good; but if salt has lost its taste, how can its saltiness be restored?
text.Rotherham: Good, therefore is the salt; but, if, even the salt, become tasteless, wherewith shall it be seasoned?

text.NRSV: It is fit neither for the soil nor for the manure pile; they throw it away. Let anyone with ears to hear listen!”
text.Rotherham: Neither for land nor for manure, is it, fit: outside, they cast it! He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.
